content:
Hi! This is something I've been wondering about for a while, because it comes up annoyingly often and is the cause of most of the boilerplate I've written in rust by hand. I guess I'm asking this in the kernel zulip first on the off chance that there's already something in the kernel for this.
The problem I'm hitting is that macro_rules! hygiene honestly seems to get in the way far more often then it helps, particularly because there are plenty of situations where I would like to pass an expression and explicitly have it evaluated during macro expansion and not at the callsite. This started with me trying to write this, rather crazy but with a reason macro:

/// Implement the repetitive from_opaque/try_from_opaque methods for all mode object and state
/// types.
///
/// Because there are so many different ways of accessing mode objects, their states, etc. we need a
/// macro that we can use for consistently implementing try_from_opaque()/from_opaque() functions to
/// convert from Opaque mode objects to fully typed mode objects. This macro handles that, and can
/// generate said functions for any kind of type which the original mode object driver trait can be
/// derived from. All conversions check the mode object's vtable. For example:
///
/// ```
/// impl<'a, T: DriverConnectorState> ConnectorState<T> {
///     impl_from_opaque_mode_obj! {
///         // | An optional lifetime and meta-variables to declare for each function
///         // |          | The type of the input parameter `opaque`
///         // |          |                               | The return type for try_from_opaque()
///         // |          |                               | from_opaque() will return &'a Self
///         // v          v                               v
///         fn <'a, D, C>(&'a OpaqueConnectorState<T>) -> Option<&'a Self>
///         where // <-- An optional set of additional trait bounds to match against
///             T: DriverConnectorState<Connector = C>
///         use
///         //  | Meta-variable that will contain ::OPS (the auto-generated vtable)
///         //  |    | The driver trait implemented by the driver for generating the vtable
///         //  |    | It will add the bound C: DriverConnector<Driver = D> to the function
///         //  v    v
///             C as DriverConnector,
///         //  | Meta-variable to assign to KmsDriver
///         //  |    | This must always be KmsDriver, it's just here for clarity
///         //  v    v
///             D as KmsDriver,
///             |o| o.connector() // The closure used for obtaining a reference to the mode object
///         return
///         //  | The value to return on success, must be a pattern like Ok(full) or Some(full)
///         //  | `full` is defined as the fully qualified type from the onversion
///         //  |        | The value to return if the conversion fails, must also be a pattern
///         //  |        | `opaque` is defined here and can be used with Err() for functions that
///         //  |        | want to return the original un-converted input value
///         //  v        v
///             Ok(full), Err(opaque)
///     }
/// }
/// ```
macro_rules! impl_from_opaque_mode_obj {
    (
        fn <
            $( $lifetime:lifetime, )?
            $( $decl_bound_id:ident ),*
        > ($opaque:ty) -> $res_container:ident<$inner_ret_ty:ty $(, $fail_ret_ty:ty )?>
        $( where $( $extra_bound_id:ident : $extra_trait:ident<$extra_assoc:ident = $extra_meta_match:ident> ),+ )?
        use
            $obj_trait_meta:ident as $obj_trait:ident,
            $drv_trait_meta:ident as KmsDriver,
            $obj_func:expr
        return $ok:expr, $fail:expr
    ) => {
        #[doc = "Try to convert `opaque` into a fully qualified `Self`."]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This will try to convert `opaque` into `Self` if it shares the same [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation as `Self`.")]
        pub fn try_from_opaque<$( $lifetime, )? $( $decl_bound_id ),* >(
            opaque: $opaque
        ) -> $res_container<$inner_ret_ty $(, $fail_ret_ty:ty )?>
        where
            $drv_trait_meta: KmsDriver,
            $obj_trait_meta: $obj_trait<Driver = $drv_trait_meta>
            $( , $( $extra_bound_id: $extra_trait<$extra_assoc = $extra_meta_match> ),+ )?
        {
            // Get the mode object `opaque` belongs to
            let obj = $obj_func(opaque);
​
            // SAFETY: The vtables for a modesetting object are initialized throughout the
            // lifetime of the object.
            let funcs = unsafe { *obj.as_raw() }.funcs;
​
            let obj_funcs = &$obj_trait_meta::OPS.funcs;
​
            if core::ptr::eq(funcs, obj_funcs) {
                // SAFETY: We only perform this transmutation if the opaque object shares our vtable
                // pointers, so the underlying full object must share our data layout.
                let full = unsafe { core::mem::transmute(opaque) };
​
                $ok
            } else {
                $fail
            }
        }
​
        #[doc = "Convert `opaque` into a fully qualified `Self`."]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This is an infallible version of [`Self::try_from_opaque`]. This ",
                        "function is mainly useful for drivers ""where only a single [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation exists.")]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = "# Panics"]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This function will panic if `opaque` belongs to a different [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation.")]
        pub fn from_opaque<$( $lifetime, )? $( $decl_bound_id ),* >(
            opaque: $opaque
        ) -> $inner_ret_ty
        where
            $drv_trait_meta: KmsDriver,
            $obj_trait_meta: $obj_trait<Driver = $drv_trait_meta>
            $( , $( $extra_bound_id: $extra_trait<$extra_assoc = $extra_meta_match> ),+ )?
        {
            Self::try_from_opaque(opaque)
                .expect(concat!("Passed ", stringify!($opaque), " does not share this ",
                                stringify!($obj_trait), " implementation."))
        }
    };
}

This macro doesn't work because if you try something like:

    /* … */
    return Ok(full), Err(opaque)

rust gets upset that it can't evaluate full or opaque at the call-site, which isn't what we want. As many devs would point out: why not use a procedural macro?

My reasoning is that I'd like to find a workaround for this because unfortunately I kind of think if the purpose of macro_rules! hygiene is to make code more legible, boiler-plate code like what's above does not in any way seem more legible as a complex block of AST-generating code rather than existing in a form that closely resembles the actual functions it's generating. I don't have anything wrong with procedural macros btw, I think they're great for a lot of things! But it really does not seem like the right tool for something as simple as this, where the boilerplate just happens to be varied enough I don't see any reasonable way of doing this without macros. So, do we have any kind of "please don't evaluate this at the callsite" escape hatch, or is anyone aware of any ongoing rust work to add support for this? Could we even try to make a case to rust upstream to add something like this?
I think that what you are trying to do is the exact reason of have macro hygiene. If the return pattern is common, could you instead have it generated inside the macro?
I think that if you supply full to the macro from the outside as an expression, it should work. It will be less ergonomic though.

macro_rules! impl_from_opaque_mode_obj {
    (
        fn <
            $( $lifetime:lifetime, )?
            $( $decl_bound_id:ident ),*
        > ($opaque:ty) -> $res_container:ident<$inner_ret_ty:ty $(, $fail_ret_ty:ty )?>
        $( where $( $extra_bound_id:ident : $extra_trait:ident<$extra_assoc:ident = $extra_meta_match:ident> ),+ )?
        use
            $obj_trait_meta:ident as $obj_trait:ident,
            $drv_trait_meta:ident as KmsDriver,
            $obj_func:expr
        ok_id $ok_id:ident,
        return $ok:expr, $fail:expr
    ) => {
        #[doc = "Try to convert `opaque` into a fully qualified `Self`."]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This will try to convert `opaque` into `Self` if it shares the same [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation as `Self`.")]
        pub fn try_from_opaque<$( $lifetime, )? $( $decl_bound_id ),* >(
            opaque: $opaque
        ) -> $res_container<$inner_ret_ty $(, $fail_ret_ty:ty )?>
        where
            $drv_trait_meta: KmsDriver,
            $obj_trait_meta: $obj_trait<Driver = $drv_trait_meta>
            $( , $( $extra_bound_id: $extra_trait<$extra_assoc = $extra_meta_match> ),+ )?
        {
            // Get the mode object `opaque` belongs to
            let obj = $obj_func(opaque);
​
            // SAFETY: The vtables for a modesetting object are initialized throughout the
            // lifetime of the object.
            let funcs = unsafe { *obj.as_raw() }.funcs;
​
            let obj_funcs = &$obj_trait_meta::OPS.funcs;
​
            if core::ptr::eq(funcs, obj_funcs) {
                // SAFETY: We only perform this transmutation if the opaque object shares our vtable
                // pointers, so the underlying full object must share our data layout.
                let $ok_id = unsafe { core::mem::transmute(opaque) };
​
                $ok
            } else {
                $fail
            }
        }
​
        #[doc = "Convert `opaque` into a fully qualified `Self`."]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This is an infallible version of [`Self::try_from_opaque`]. This ",
                        "function is mainly useful for drivers ""where only a single [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation exists.")]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = "# Panics"]
        #[doc = ""]
        #[doc = concat!("This function will panic if `opaque` belongs to a different [`",
                        stringify!($obj_trait), "`] implementation.")]
        pub fn from_opaque<$( $lifetime, )? $( $decl_bound_id ),* >(
            opaque: $opaque
        ) -> $inner_ret_ty
        where
            $drv_trait_meta: KmsDriver,
            $obj_trait_meta: $obj_trait<Driver = $drv_trait_meta>
            $( , $( $extra_bound_id: $extra_trait<$extra_assoc = $extra_meta_match> ),+ )?
        {
            Self::try_from_opaque(opaque)
                .expect(concat!("Passed ", stringify!($opaque), " does not share this ",
                                stringify!($obj_trait), " implementation."))
        }
    };
}

In this case, I usually use exactly what Andreas Hindborg suggested. Maybe you can also use closures, so for example: return (|full| Ok(full)), (|opaque| Err(opaque)). And then in the expansion use $ok(full).

Lyude said:

My reasoning is that I'd like to find a workaround for this because unfortunately I kind of think if the purpose of macro_rules! hygiene is to make code more legible, boiler-plate code like what's above does not in any way seem more legible as a complex block of AST-generating code rather than existing in a form that closely resembles the actual functions it's generating.

The reason for macro hygiene is not to make code more legible, but to prevent that macros aren't general enough. So for example you have a macro:

macro_rules! foo {
    ($value:expr) => {
        let mut foo = 0;
        foo += $value;
        println!("{foo}");
    }
}
// then you can use it like this:
let x = 42;
foo!(x); // this prints 42

// but if we had no hygiene, then this would be different:
let foo = 42;
foo!(foo); // this prints 0!

So essentially macro hygiene prevents renaming of variables, constants and functions from affecting program behavior. It is very important, as this is a huge problem in macro systems that don't have it, because you have to name all of your in-macro variables "__macro_internal_var..." (and then still you have the problem of what happens if a macro-generated variable is input into a macro...).

I don't have anything wrong with procedural macros btw, I think they're great for a lot of things! But it really does not seem like the right tool for something as simple as this, where the boilerplate just happens to be varied enough I don't see any reasonable way of doing this without macros. So, do we have any kind of "please don't evaluate this at the callsite" escape hatch, or is anyone aware of any ongoing rust work to add support for this? Could we even try to make a case to rust upstream to add something like this?

Yeah agreed, for this a declarative macro should suffice.

Benno Lossin said:

In this case, I usually use exactly what Andreas Hindborg suggested. Maybe you can also use closures, so for example: return (|full| Ok(full)), (|opaque| Err(opaque)). And then in the expansion use $ok(full).

Ah, that is neat :+1:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think that if you supply full to the macro from the outside as an expression, it should work. It will be less ergonomic though.

Huh. So using Ok(full) as an example here, simply passing full as an :ident token in the macro would make it not have an issue with Ok(full) in $ok:expr? I would think that since full is being declared in the macro arguments and not at the call-site it would still lead to the same issue

Lyude said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think that if you supply full to the macro from the outside as an expression, it should work. It will be less ergonomic though.

Huh. So using Ok(full) as an example here, simply passing full as an :ident token in the macro would make it not have an issue with Ok(full) in $ok:expr? I would think that since full is being declared in the macro arguments and not at the call-site it would still lead to the same issue

I think it would be OK. The compiler assigns a parse context to the identifier. If the fullidentifier is created in the macro context, the return expression you pass in cannot reference it. But if the context of full is the outside context, you should be able to reference it from the return expression you pass in. But you would have to let $full_id = ... in the macro.
There is a good explanation of this somewhere on the internet, where they use colors to communicate the contexts of the identifiers. I'll see if I can find it.
This one. It was literally the first hit for me on google for the search phrase "rust macro syntax hygiene".
